Chart the System.

Healthcare is a web of clinical, operational, financial, and technical dependencies. This is the system map — built for people who need to see more than their own slice.

Recent Additions

The latest talks, articles, and tools added to the site.

CME Credit May 1, 2026
AI in Healthcare: Value vs. Hype — ArMA Annual Meeting 2026
Talk page and downloads from David Eitel's ArMA 2026 presentation. Includes the 5-question evaluation framework, all source references, and CME details.
→
CME Credit Apr 9, 2026
AI in Healthcare: Operational Value, Limitations, and Oversight — MSRC 2026
Talk page and downloads from David Eitel's MSRC 2026 presentation on AI in healthcare — the Operations vs. Clinical AI framework, insurance AI, and RT practice implications.
→

Clinical SQL Mystery
Learn healthcare data models by solving real clinical mysteries with SQL queries.
→
Featured Oct 19, 2025
Healthcare Vendor Directory
A searchable, filterable directory of major healthcare technology vendors — what sector they operate in, which 4Ps layers they touch, and whether their technology is proven, emerging, or cautionary.
→
New Here?

Three ways into the system

Whether you work at the bedside, in analytics, or in a boardroom — find your layer of the map.

01
Path 01 · For Clinicians
I Work in Healthcare
You use an enterprise EHR every shift but want to understand why data is structured the way it is — and why your documentation matters beyond patient care.
Start with the EHR explainer →
02
Path 02 · For Analysts & Informaticists
I Work with Healthcare Data
You write SQL, pull reports, or work with healthcare datasets and need the clinical context behind the tables — what an encounter is, how patient identity works, and why reporting lags exist.
Try the SQL Mystery Game →
03
Path 03 · For Leaders & Strategists
I Make Technology Decisions
You are evaluating AI platforms, interoperability initiatives, or EHR implementations and need an honest picture of what the data shows and where the real gaps are.
Explore the AI Healthcare Map →

1987
Year HL7 was founded — the standard that made EHR interoperability possible
HL7 International · About HL7
$25.9B
HITECH Act EHR incentive payments that drove nationwide adoption from 2009 forward
HHS.gov · HITECH Act
~36%
U.S. hospital market share held by the largest single EHR vendor by bed count
KLAS Research · 2024 EHR Rankings
157
Defined FHIR R4 resource types — the open standard for healthcare data exchange
HL7 FHIR R4 · Resource List
Page Sources
  • HL7 International — About HL7
  • HL7 FHIR R4 Resource List
  • HHS — HITECH Act Rule
  • ONC — Federal HIT Strategic Plan
  • KLAS Research — EHR Market Data
  • Epic — Public FHIR API Docs
  • Oracle Health (Cerner) — Public FHIR Docs
David Eitel
RRT · MHA
MSRT · RRT-ACCS
About Healthcare Uncharted

Healthcare technology is full of territory that nobody maps in plain language. Clinicians use enterprise EHRs every day without understanding why the data is structured the way it is. Analysts write reports without knowing the clinical meaning behind the tables. Leaders make AI investments without knowing what actually works versus what is still emerging.

Healthcare Uncharted exists to close that gap. This is a living system map built from real clinical and informatics experience — interactive tools, deep-dive articles, terminology guides, and honest assessments of where healthcare technology actually stands.

Built by a Respiratory Therapist turned healthcare informaticist. The goal is simple: map the connections, not just the components — for every role in the system, not just the technical ones.

Respiratory Therapy Health Administration Clinical Informatics EHR Architecture Health Data Standards AI in Healthcare
David Eitel · RRT, MHA, MSRT, RRT-ACCS · Senior Clinical Informatics Analyst at Intermountain Health
Learn more →
A living resource for clinical informatics, healthcare data architecture, and the real story behind the systems that run modern medicine.

Built by David Eitel, RRT, MHA, MSRT, RRT-ACCS

Source Policy
All vendor-specific terminology, technical specifications, and system references on this site are derived from publicly available documentation — including fhir.epic.com, fhir.cerner.com, HL7 FHIR R4, ONC HealthIT.gov, and HHS.gov. Each factual claim is linked to its source. This site does not reproduce proprietary materials, internal vendor training content, or licensed documentation.
